meta.title: 440i Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: BMW 440i B58 3.0 engine — real UK costs, oil filter housing & cooling system insight, and honest reliability data. Get free quotes from 20+ vetted BMW specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 440i engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned B58 3.0-litre engine typically costs £4,500–£7,500 supply only. Used engines are available from £3,500–£5,500. Fitting adds £1,200–£1,800 depending on your location and workshop. Main dealer new engines can exceed £10,000.

Yes — the B58 is widely considered one of BMW's most reliable modern engines. It's a significant step forward from the N55, with improved cooling, durability, and reduced friction. However, it has known weak points: oil filter housing leaks, water pump failures, PCV diaphragm issues, and — on early models — plastic oil pump components. Regular maintenance is essential.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 440i?

acceptedAnswer.text: The most frequently reported issues are oil filter housing leaks (plastic housing cracks, causing oil and coolant to mix), water pump and thermostat failures, PCV valve diaphragm failure (causing rough idle and smoke), and VANOS solenoid wear. Early B58 engines (pre-2020) also had plastic oil pump components that could fail catastrophically.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW 440i engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and which component has failed. Against a well-kept £25,000 440i, a £6,000 recon is 24% — worth it. Against a tired £10,000 example, the same recon approaches our 70% BMW threshold — get a scrap valuation first.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 440i engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The 440i carries the engine code B58B30M0 (pre-LCI) or B58B30O1 (LCI models). Both are 3.0-litre straight-six turbo petrol engines. The B58 is part of BMW's modular engine family.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the oil filter housing leak, or does it need full engine replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — oil filter housing replacement is a well-understood repair costing £500–£1,200. The housing is made of plastic and is prone to cracking. If the housing fails, oil and coolant can mix, leading to catastrophic engine damage. An aftermarket metal housing upgrade is available for a permanent fix. If you notice oil spots or a burning smell, have it inspected immediately.

problems.title: Oil Filter Housing Leaks
problems.symptoms: Oil spots under the car, burning oil smell from the engine bay, low oil level warning, visible oil residue around the oil filter housing area.
problems.repairCost: £500–£1,200 (oil filter housing replacement)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.recommendation: The B58's oil filter housing is made of plastic and is prone to cracking and leaking. If the housing fails, oil and coolant can mix — a potentially catastrophic failure. If you notice oil spots or a burning smell, have the oil filter housing inspected promptly. An aftermarket metal housing upgrade is available.

failureLink.label: Read the full oil leak guide

problems.title: Water Pump & Cooling System Failures
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss, overheating, temperature gauge fluctuations, engine warning light, sweet smell from the engine bay.
problems.repairCost: £400–£900 (water pump replacement)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.recommendation: The B58's water pump and thermostat are prone to premature failure. The plastic impellers wear out faster than metal ones. If you're experiencing coolant loss or overheating, have the cooling system inspected promptly. An upgraded water pump with metal impellers improves reliability.

failureLink.label: Read the full coolant leak guide

problems.title: PCV Valve Diaphragm Failure
problems.symptoms: Rough idle, whistling noise from the engine, white or blue smoke from exhaust on startup, oil in the intake/chargepipe, misfires.
problems.repairCost: £200–£500 (PCV diaphragm repair kit)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.recommendation: The PCV diaphragm on first-generation B58 engines can tear, causing high crankcase vacuum and smoking. The good news is this is easily fixed with an affordable repair kit. If you notice rough idle, whistling, or smoke on startup, have the PCV system inspected.

failureLink.label: Read the full PCV failure guide

buyingChecks.title: Buying This 440i? Check These First
buyIf: * Full BMW service history with evidence of regular oil changes using BMW LL-01 approved oil
* Post-2020 build (metal oil pump fitted from factory)
* No coolant loss or overheating history
* No rough idle, whistling, or smoke on startup
avoidIf: * Oil leaks with no documented oil filter housing inspection or replacement
* Coolant loss with no cooling system service records
* Rough idle or whistling from the engine (PCV diaphragm warning)
* Engine warning light with no diagnosis

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The 440i's B58 is one of BMW's finest modern engines — a genuinely robust, powerful, and tunable straight-six that represents a significant step forward from the N55. It's not bulletproof — oil filter housing leaks, water pump failures, and PCV diaphragm issues are the price of entry — but these are well-understood, manageable fixes. Buy on documented history, not on faith. A well-maintained 440i is a £20,000–£35,000 future classic; a neglected one is a £7,000 engine replacement waiting to happen.

cta.label: → Compare 440i engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW 440i (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rough idle, whistling, or smoke (PCV diaphragm warning)
* Service History — including the B58 era (2016–2020) and confirmed oil-change intervals using BMW LL-01 approved oil
* Oil Leak Check — Any history of oil leaks? (oil filter housing is a known weak point)
* Coolant Check — Any history of coolant loss or water pump/thermostat replacement?
* Smoke Check — White or blue smoke on startup can indicate PCV or turbo issues
* VANOS Operation — Any hesitation, rough idle, or loss of power? (VANOS solenoid warning)
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved VANOS, cooling system, or PCV issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
